speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Lesley Nathan. The date is January 16th, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Barbara Marlow. The location is London, England. My name is Leslie Nathan. The date is January 16th, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Barbara Marlow. The location is London, England. Barbara, could you tell me your name and the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Barbara Marlow. M-A-R-L-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me your name at birth and the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born Barbara Berta Hagina Bregman. B-R-E-G-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on Sunday, September 21st, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now age 66.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you now begin to tell me the story of your life from the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the younger of two children. My brother, George, was eight years older than myself. And when I was born, everybody was delighted to have a little girl in the family again, as my grandparents had one daughter who died at the age of a year of meningitis. Afterwards, they had two sons. And they were very happy to have a little girl in the family. My father, Leon, was working in the petroleum industry. He had his own company, which was trading with various companies, including one in England. He had graduated in law, but he never practiced as a lawyer. We had a comfortable, middle class life. We lived in the center of Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My-- we were a close family. I really didn't know my maternal grandfather, who died when my mother was very young. Her mother was very elderly, as she had a family of five children, of whom my mother was the youngest. And her eldest sister already had children of my mother's age by the time she arrived. For this reason, I was much closer to my father's family. My grandparents lived not far from us, also in the center of Warsaw. My grandfather was a neurologist at the Jewish hospital. He was head of department, and one of the very known-- well-known Polish neurologist who published a lot in the international medical press, in France, in Germany, as well as in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I also knew my great-grandfather, Sebastian Bregman, who died in 1939. He was very involved in social work. At one stage, he was president of the Jewish Council in Warsaw. My father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a great Polish patriot. He fought for the liberation of Poland in the 1918-1920 war under Marshal Pilsudski. And after the war, he was involved with the Jewish ex-Combatants Association, where he was one of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] top members of the organization. In 1935, he was elected to the Council of Warsaw, on which he served up to the beginning of the war. He's-- we were brought up to think that we were of Polish nationality and the Jewish faith. And that was our attitude throughout. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I really was not aware of any distinction between myself and my friends. I went to school in 1937, to a private school. The only thing I remember was being cross when my friends were taking first communion in the pretty white dresses, and couldn't quite understand why I couldn't have the same. My contact with the Jewish faith was-- consisted of visits to the synagogue on special days to take flowers to my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Cheder, to Jewish Sunday school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I know that my brother was bar mitzvah. But that-- I would have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: About five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it was eight years difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was eight years older. So at 13, I would have been 5 years old. And I really don't remember anything of that. I also remember that, once a year, I was allowed to stay up late and we went to my great-grandfather's for Passover. That was big occasion for me, as he was very old, but quite an amazing person. I used to love sitting on his lap and plaiting his beard. Everybody would get very excited and say, leave Grandpa alone. And he would say, let the child be. And that's my memory of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've talked about your father. Can you tell us something about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother also did quite a bit of social work, together with my grandmother. They were friends of children-- Jewish Children's Hospital. And each year, there was a big ball where a cot, a doll's cot, was auctioned for the benefit of the hospital. And that was another sort of bone of contention, because I was told that I could never win that cot being part of the organization, so to speak. And I used to get very upset over it because it was always very beautiful. My mother did some work for girls who were handicapped. And they used to make aprons and caps for maids. And I remember those things being cut out on our dining room table, the ladies coming around to see to it. And then they were made up into caps and aprons for the maids of those days. And models would be sent over from Paris, and it was quite exciting.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk about going to school when you were seven. What sort of schooling did you have before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Poland, children don't start school until you are seven. Before that, I had a governess, who used to-- well, I had a nanny up to the age of probably four or five. Then I had a governess who came in daily. At that stage, it was a French governess. Again, because of the Polish attitudes, we did not have a German nanny or a German governess, which was more or less the norm in a lot of Jewish families. But I had a French governess. And that's been very useful because it meant that I'm virtually bilingual in Polish and French.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she like, this governess?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a French-- a French-Swiss lady called Miss-- oh, god, what was her name? Soshka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was an elderly spinster. And she used to come every morning and take me for my walk. And then she was there at lunch time. And I remember my father coming from the office for his lunch. And the idea was that everybody would be speaking French, including my brother and my father. And Father saying to her, I'll pay you extra. Don't speak today. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's one of the sort of memories. My brother, at that time, was already in secondary school. He had passed the competitive entrance exam for gymnasium number one in Warsaw, the [NON-ENGLISH] gymnasium, which was the best public school. By public, I mean as opposed to private in Warsaw. And it was very prestigious. There were-- the children there-- it was for boys only. And they were the boys of government officials, and military, and intellectuals.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was very happy there. I think there was another Jewish boy. I can't-- I think it was Richard Behovski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I don't think that George had any problems from what I know. And he made very many friends, some of whom helped us during the war. In particular, one family, but we'll come to that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your own friends before you went to school, how did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think most of my friends were Jewish, but similar to myself, very assimilated. And we all spoke Polish. We never spoke anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear Yiddish in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandparents speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They spoke German when I wasn't supposed to understand. But it was pure German. In fact, my father spoke excellent German. Whenever he was after the war in Germany, people wouldn't believe that he wasn't German because he spoke with what they called Berliner accent.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your home did you celebrate Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So really having Seder night at your grandparents was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't celebrate Shabbat either.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still celebrated Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other festivals?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I didn't think so. I mean, I can't remember what was the occasions when I would have gone to the synagogue to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, perhaps at Yom Kippur, did your parents go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't-- I really don't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us more about the sort of things you did as a family? Was it a musical household?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Sadly, we are not musical at all. No, we used to go for walks together on Sundays. Other than that, I think, because of the age difference with my brother, who was eight years older than myself, we had different activities and different interests.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: We always used to go-- we always used to go on Sundays for lunch at my grandparents'. And each of us, my brother and myself, had one day in the week when we went to have lunch on our own. And that was, again, great fun because we were allowed to choose the menu for our weekly lunch. I went to school, I did skating, swimming, played with my friends, went to children parties, that sort of thing, that sort of ordinary, normal childhood pursuits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were at school, when you were seven, did you have Jewish and non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't answer that. I didn't look in those terms. I just had friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't differentiate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't differentiate between them. Jewishness really wasn't something which mattered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever see anti-Semitism in your school or around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I never came across any. Or perhaps I wasn't aware of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You could say that I was sheltered. But I think living in the center of Warsaw-- I didn't have any friends who lived in the sort of Jewish part of Warsaw. All my friends lived in the center of Warsaw. So I really didn't have much contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I liked it very much, but I only had two years of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So perhaps you can tell us what happened when war broke out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were there any signs in your family that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think there was a lot of talk, but it didn't really involve me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were nine when war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was eight at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know that war was expected. That summer-- summer of 1939, in August-- I went with my mother to a mountain resort of Zakopane. My father stayed in Warsaw. My brother went with my aunt and uncle to the Masurian Lakes, sailing. And towards the end of the month, there were more and more rumors of war. And finally-- I think it was about the 25th-- my father telephoned and said that we were to return to Warsaw immediately. Well, we got on the train, we got to Krakow, where we were met by some friends who lived there, who persuaded my mother to stay overnight so that I could see the sights. I had never been to Krakow before-- which we did. And, obviously, my father wasn't very pleased about it. Because, the next day, we were visiting Wawel Castle, I suddenly saw him striding in and saying we had to leave straight away because things were getting very hairy. And it will be very difficult to get back to Warsaw. And already the stations were full of soldiers and people trying to get on the trains. The trains were terribly crowded. And Father went on to the sidings to get a place for us in the train going to Warsaw. When we arrived-- must have been about the 28th, 29th of August-- it was decided that I would go and join my grandparents, who were on holiday outside Warsaw. So I went to stay with them while my parents and my brother were still in Warsaw. And then they-- I remember the 1st of September. We were in a sort of hotel boarding house. And I went downstairs and everybody was there listening to the radio and saying war had started. And, in fact, one of the-- the first raid on Warsaw, the Germans, by mistake, dropped bombs very near where we were. So I saw virtually one of the first bombs of the Second World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your feelings as a child about what was going on around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared, but I was also excited. I mean, I didn't know what war meant. It was a new experience. And then, my father, brother, and mother, and brother came to see us on the Sunday. I can't remember what date it was. And there was a lot of talk of what was going to happen. And Father was trying to-- he was an officer in the reserve. He was trying to join the army, but things were rather disorganized at that stage. And my brother, who was a Boy Scout, he wanted to join. He was only 17 at the time. And then-- must have been about the 5th-- we went back to Warsaw. And on the 7th, the president of the city of Warsaw asked all men to leave the city and declared Warsaw an open city, hoping that that would save it from the ravages of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father and my brother left that night, and Mother and myself moved in with my grandparents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, of course, we didn't hear anything more about them what had happened. And then the bombing of Warsaw started-- open city or no open city. And that was pretty frightening. I was petrified. I remember the bombs just raining down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandparents lived very near the tallest building in Warsaw. It was considered-- called a skyscraper, the Prudential Skyscraper. But, by today's standards, it certainly wasn't. I think it had something like 14 stories, or maybe 20. And so we moved into the next street, where my grandmother's brother lived. And we all sat in the cellars there. And when the bombing stopped at night, we used to go out. And it was frightening because a lot of Warsaw was burning, so there were fires all around. And you could see, in the square, there were dead horses lying there. It really was very, very frightening. And the bombing went on, and on, and on. I think Warsaw didn't surrender until the 26th of September. So we had about three weeks before we could surface and go back. Well, my grandparents' flat was not damaged, but our own flat was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it turned out that I contracted scarlet fever during the time we were in the cellars, when everybody was mixing with everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was taken ill, I think either at the end of September, beginning of October. And it was a notifiable disease, so the German authorities had to be notified. But as my grandfather was a doctor, and at that time the Germans were still behaving quite well. They came for inspection to grandfather's flat, where my old nurse was looking after me and all the proper sanitary precautions were taken. And they still called my grandfather Herr Kollege. Did they know you were a Jewish family at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. But, at that stage, it didn't make much difference. And then, at the beginning of October, both my father and my brother returned to Warsaw. It was the day when Hitler was taking the parades in Warsaw, the victory parade. And suddenly, they both turned up, one after the other. My father had gone quite far east when Poland surrendered, and he decided to come back home. My brother was taken prisoner by the Germans with his colleagues in Scouts, but, with the help of a Polish peasant, escaped from the POW camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen? How did he escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, while he was-- a peasant gave him clothes, and he walked out of the camp. So they both came to Warsaw, to my mother's great dismay. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because, at that stage, one already knew that there were various rules and regulations as far as the Jews were concerned. And I think probably about that, soon after, the Jewish star was introduced. And my brother hated it. So half the time, he wouldn't wear anything with a Jewish star. And every time he passed a German in the street, he gave him a look as if he could kill him. So things were rather difficult. Also, my father's name, as a counselor of the city of Warsaw, and involved in other organizations-- Jewish ex-Combatants and so on-- his name was on various lists. And the Germans had been asking where he was. So Mother was very, very worried. And then, in November, we got notification from the Romanian embassy that they were holding visas for Romania for my father and my brother. What had happened was that my uncle, who was a journalist, left with the Polish government in September of '39 and went to Romania. And realizing what would be happening, arranged for visas for them. He didn't think at that time that my mother and myself would be at risk, so he just got the two visas. Well, my father was not keen on leaving us behind. And I believe that he purchased some South American visas for us to leave as well. He had no intention of going to Romania. He wanted to rejoin the Polish army, which we heard had been formed in France. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And all went reasonably well. He had to go to the Gestapo to-- to be interviewed. And despite the wonderful German bureaucracy, while he was at the Gestapo headquarters, they telephoned the Archives Department to check on him, and somehow missed. So he was given a passport. But, to get your visa-- exit visa, it had to be stamped by the governor of the occupied territory, who was stationed in Krakow. It was General Frank. And to get that required a bribe. There were specialists who used to take passports to be stamped. And I remember that everything was already packed, my father's cases and our own cases. And I had my favorite Shirley Temple doll dressed in traveling clothes. The German customs had been to the flat to stamp the cases. When the specialist came back and said that things weren't quite right, and he was afraid of giving three passports in the same name to be stamped on one occasion. So he decided that my father's and my brother's passport were the most important ones, but that he would get ours stamped the following week. My father was all for waiting for us to leave together, but Mother was so anxious, felt that, every day, they were in danger. She insisted that they should leave. And they left on the 6th of April. Well, things did not work out the way it was planned. Because, somehow, our passports didn't come until much later, by which time the German offensive on Holland and Belgium had started. And then, my grandparents persuaded my mother that it would be dangerous for her to start traveling at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's how it came that we stayed behind, and we never left Poland. Not till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after your father and your brother left, what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just stayed on at our grandparents'.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice any difference in the way people around you treated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. The only difference was that Germans cut off open house. They used to come and take away whatever they felt like taking away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did they take anything from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took-- they would come to the flat and walk around, as if it were a department store. Have a look and say, you know, they would feel like taking this carpet or this picture. My grandfather's equipment, because he had his surgery at home, and he had x-ray, and I don't know what other medical machinery-- now that was taken away quite early. I remember two occasions. On the one occasion, they came into the kitchen with a-- there was a collection of saucepans. And the soldier who was taking them away got very excited, because they were made in a factory in his hometown. And he was very polite. Copper was required for the war effort, but all that went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. That was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I just remembered saucepans and him being very polite to my grandmother, calling her [NON-ENGLISH]. And, at that stage, they behaved. It didn't happen on other occasions.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
